coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of paulownia flower-derived carbon dots for sensitive fluorescent detection of paclobutrazol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yi
Dong, Ping
Sun, Nina
Wang, Xu
She, Xinshuai
Xu, Yongqian
Wang, Qiongzi
Zhang, Yun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dots derived from paulownia flowers (PF-CDs) were synthesized and utilized as a nanosensor for the highly sensitive detection of paclobutrazol (PBZ) pesticide residue. The study reports the first green synthesis of PF-CDs using biological waste from PF through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. PF-CDs exhibited exceptional photostability, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, making them suitable for biological applications. Moreover, PF-CDs exhibited excellent sensitivity for detecting PBZ pesticides. A strong linear correlation (R2 > 0.99) was observed between the fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs and PBZ concentrations ranging from 0.78 to 18.75 μg/L. In real sample analysis, PBZ recovery rates ranged from 94.06 % to 108.56 %, with relative standard deviations between 0.35 % and 12.35 %. These results highlight the potential of PF-CDs for the accurate and reliable detection of pesticide residues. This study provides a sensitive method for PBZ detection, with implications for food safety and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Carbon dots
Paulownia flowers
Pesticide residue detection
Paclobutrazol
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of the PF-CDs probe in samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Higher Education Institutions in Henan Province (Grant No. 25B330001), the Key Scientific Research Projects of Henan Province (No. 222102310688), the 111 Project (No. D20036), and the start-up fund from Xinxiang Medical University (Grant No. 201905).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FETEM image of PF-CDs, inset shows the average particle size of PF-CDs. (b) HRFETEM image of PF-CDs, inset shows the inter-planar distance of PF-CDs.
(a) FTIR of PF-CDs, (b) XRD of PF-CDs, (c) XPS survey spectra of PF-CDs, (d) C1s peaks of PF-CDs, (e) O1s peaks of PF-CDs, (f) N1s peaks of PF-CDs.

(a) and (b) Fluorescence spectra of PF-CDs under excitation wavelengths at 240–400 nm, (c) Excitation and emission spectra of PF-CDs, (d) Fluorescence spectra of PF-CDs with different precursor concentrations.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs with different PF-CDs concentrations, (b) Fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs under irradiating for 2 h, (c) Effect of the NaCl concentrations on fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs, (d) Fluorescence spectra within the pH range of 1–11.

(a) Effect of the PF-CDs concentration on fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs-PBZ system, (b) Various buffer solutions on the fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs-PBZ system, (c) Dosage of Na2HPO4-citric acid buffer solution on the fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs-PBZ system.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of the PF-CDs-PBZ system at various paclobutrazol concentrations, (b) linear relationship between ΔF and paclobutrazol concentration.
(a) Livability of BEAS2B cells incubated at different PF-CDs concentrations, (b) Livability of A549 cells incubated at different PF-CDs concentrations.

(a) brightfield image of BMDM cells, (b) Blue light of PF-CDs-stained BMDM cells. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The preparation process and detection principle of PF-CDs.
Comparison between previously reported sensors for the detection of paclobutrazol.
PF-CDs-PBZ system assay in actual samples.
Effects of various amino acids interferential substances on the PF-CDs-PBZ system.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of paulownia flower-derived carbon dots for sensitive fluorescent detection of paclobutrazol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Technology, Xinxiang Medical University, Henan, Xinxiang 453003, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medical Technology, Xinxiang Medical University, Henan Xinxiang 453003, P.R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contribute equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon dots derived from paulownia flowers (PF-CDs) were synthesized and utilized as a nanosensor for the highly sensitive detection of paclobutrazol (PBZ) pesticide residue. The study reports the first green synthesis of PF-CDs using biological waste from PF through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. PF-CDs exhibited exceptional photostability, low cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, making them suitable for biological applications. Moreover, PF-CDs exhibited excellent sensitivity for detecting PBZ pesticides. A strong linear correlation (R2 > 0.99) was observed between the fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs and PBZ concentrations ranging from 0.78 to 18.75 μg/L. In real sample analysis, PBZ recovery rates ranged from 94.06 % to 108.56 %, with relative standard deviations between 0.35 % and 12.35 %. These results highlight the potential of PF-CDs for the accurate and reliable detection of pesticide residues. This study provides a sensitive method for PBZ detection, with implications for food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Paclobutrazol (PBZ) is a triazole-based plant growth regulator that effectively inhibits the synthesis of endogenous gibberellins (Zhai et al., 2013). Additionally, PBZ enhances the activity of indoleacetic acid oxidase and reduces endogenous auxin levels. PBZ is widely used in agriculture owing to its ability to delay plant growth, hinder stem elongation, and increase crop yields (Maheshwari et al., 2022). PBZ is typically applied directly to the soil and remains stable for up to two years at room temperature. However, improper application or excessive use of PBZ can result in persistent residues in the soil, which may affect crops grown on the same site in subsequent years (Sharma & Awasthi, 2005). The overuse of PBZ can disrupt soil microbial communities, which significantly hinders the growth of future crops and compromises microbial activity (Silva et al., 2003). PBZ residues in the soil can be absorbed by plants through their roots, leading to the accumulation of excessive pesticide residues in vegetables and fruits (Jiang et al., 2019). Moreover, PBZ poses significant risks to human and animal health. Upon ingestion, PBZ can cause poisoning. Studies have shown that PBZ inhibits monoamine oxidase activity, which increases dopamine levels and disrupts neurotransmitter balance in the serum (Husain et al., 2009; Xu & Yang, 2020). Exposure to PBZ may alter both cholinergic and noncholinergic neurotransmission systems in the human body (Xu & Yang, 2020).

Owing to the severe toxicity of PBZ, a maximum residue limit of 0.05 mg/kg has been established worldwide for vegetables and fruits (Bellisai et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2019). Current studies have reported various methods for determining PBZ residues. These include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Dai et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2019; Sancho et al., 2003; Xu et al., 2020), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Jiang et al., 2019; Sullivan et al., 2013), and high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) (Fan et al., 2011; Xu et al., 2020). Despite their high precision, these methods require advanced equipment, complex operational procedures, and significant resources, making them unsuitable for the efficient, high-throughput screening of various environmental samples.

Fluorescent probe-based sensors provide an effective and sensitive approach for detecting pesticide residues. These sensors can alter their fluorescence signals in response to the presence and concentration of the analyte (Cao et al., 2020). Carbon dots (CDs) are monodisperse spherical nanoparticles with an average size of less than 10 nm (Sun et al., 2006). CDs consist of a carbogenic core with various functional groups (such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups) attached to the carbon surface (Wang et al., 2017). CDs, an emerging fluorescent nanoprobe material, address several limitations of traditional probes owing to their excellent fluorescence properties, chemical stability, photostability, low cytotoxicity, and environmental friendliness (Niu et al., 2017). These properties enable CDs to emit bright and stable signals, making them highly sensitive and reliable for detection tasks (Sun et al., 2020). The sources and synthetic routes for CDs are diverse. Various synthetic methods have been reported for CD synthesis, including hydrothermal synthesis, carbonization, sonication, pyrolysis, and microwave irradiation (Das et al., 2018; de Medeiros et al., 2019; Meng et al., 2019). CDs can be synthesized from various organic molecules (including carbohydrates and citric acid) and natural biomass (such as fruits and vegetables). Biomass waste containing valuable components such as cellulose, starch, glucose, and proteins provides a cost-effective, resource-efficient, and eco-friendly method for synthesizing CDs (Fan et al., 2022).

Paulownia is a perennial woody plant native to China and Southeast Asia and has been recently introduced to various countries (Guo et al., 2023). Paulownia exhibits rapid growth and remarkable regenerative abilities owing to its adaptability to diverse environmental conditions. Within a year, paulownia can produce significantly higher biomass than slower-growing species (Jakubowski et al., 2018; Woźniak et al., 2019). Moreover, paulownia is abundant in prenylated and geranylated flavonoids, phenolic compounds, and polysaccharides, which are mainly found in the leaves, flowers, and fruits (Guo et al., 2023; Rodríguez-Seoane et al., 2020). These characteristics have led to increased interest in using paulownia for sustainable biomass energy. Paulownia flowers (PF), rich in bioactive compounds, have been used to prepare porous carbon materials (Chang et al., 2015) and as precursors for hierarchically porous activated carbon (Liu et al., 2016). The bioactive components, particularly flavonoids and phenolic compounds, significantly contribute to the formation of hierarchically porous carbon materials and the synthesis of fluorescent CDs. Wang et al. used paulownia leaf biomass waste as a carbon source to prepare CDs through a simple hydrothermal process (Wang et al., 2019). However, research on the synthesis of fluorescent sensors using PF as precursors for CDs is limited.

This study introduces a simple one-step hydrothermal method for synthesizing PF-CDs from the bioactive components of PF. The study reports the first use of PF as a precursor for CD synthesis. Compared with traditional methods such as high-temperature pyrolysis or complex chemical processes, the one-step hydrothermal synthesis is simpler, more cost-effective, easier to control, and highly suitable for large-scale applications (Das et al., 2018; Meng et al., 2019). The resulting PF-CDs exhibit low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, ensuring their safety for use in biological systems and food samples. This enables the sensitive and selective detection of PBZ residues. Moreover, this study represents the first use of a CDs-based sensor for PBZ residue analysis. Although CDs have been widely used to detect various pesticides, such as organophosphorus and carbamate pesticides (Chu et al., 2020; Mishra et al., 2022), their application in detecting PBZ has not been investigated.

This study found that PF-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity for detecting PBZ pesticides. The fluorescence response of the PF-CDs-PBZ system displayed a linear relationship with PBZ concentrations of 0.78–18.75 μg/L, with correlation coefficients exceeding 0.99. This study aims to develop an eco-friendly, cost-effective, and highly sensitive fluorescent probe for the precise detection of PBZ pesticide residues in complex environmental and food samples using PF-CDs. This method provides a sustainable alternative for synthesizing CDs and detecting pesticide residues. The findings present valuable guidance for ensuring food safety and safeguarding human health.

PF was selected as a precursor for preparing PF-CDs. PBZ was obtained from the Henan Academy of Agricultural Sciences. A PBZ stock solution (50 μg/mL) was prepared and stored at 4 °C in the dark. Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions with varying pH values of 1–11 were used to adjust the experimental conditions at room temperature. L-arginine, DL-arginine, DL-cysteine, L-cystine, and L-tert-leucin were purchased from Adamas-beta. BEAS2B (human lung epithelial), A549 (human lung carcinoma tissue), and BMDM cells (mouse bone marrow-derived macrophages) were obtained from the School of Medical Technology(Xinxiang Medical University). These cells were used to assess the cytotoxicity of PF-CDs and image living cells. Homemade apple juice, freshly squeezed from locally sourced apples, served as the experimental sample. Ultrapure water was used as the solvent throughout the experiments. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The morphology and average particle size of PF-CDs were examined via field emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA) at 200 KV. The functional groups and various bonds in PF-CDs were identified via Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Nicolet iS5, USA). The elemental composition and molecular structure of PF-CDs were analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi, USA). The crystal structure of PF-CDs was characterized via X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of the PF-CDs solution were recorded using a fluorospectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra of the aqueous PF-CD solution were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer with a 200–600 nm wavelength (UV-2700, SHIMADZU, Japan). High performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS, Agilent 1290–6460, USA) was used for further sample analysis. The HPLC system utilized a column (InfinityLab Poroshell 120EC-C18 column, 2.1 mm × 100 mm sand a 2.7 μm particle size) from Agilent Technologies (Santa Clara, CA, USA). The fluorescence lifetime of the CD solution was measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (FlS-980, Edinburgh Instrument, UK).

PF was collected from biological waste, thoroughly washed with clean water to remove dust and impurities, and then dried. The dried flowers were ground into powder and subjected to hydrothermal treatment to synthesize water-soluble PF-CDs. The synthesis parameters, including reactant dosage, reaction time, and temperature, were optimized. Specifically, 0.35 g powder of PF was dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water and transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and heated to 200 °C for 30 min. During the reaction, pressure was self-generated owing to the high-temperature sealed environment. After the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The resulting mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles and unreacted materials. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore cellulose membrane filter to obtain a clear PF-CDs solution. The stock solution of PF-CDs (16.5 mg/mL) was prepared and stored at 4 °C for subsequent spectroscopic characterization and testing.

Unless otherwise specified, the optimal conditions for PBZ detection were as follows: A 4 mL aqueous mixture was prepared through the addition of the PBZ standard stock solution to the PF-CD dispersion containing 12.5 % optimal buffer. The PF-CDs concentration was maintained at 41.25 μg/mL, while PBZ concentrations ranged from 0.78 to 18.75 μg/L. After the PF-CDs and PBZ solutions were mixed, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 260 nm. A standard calibration curve was constructed using the resulting data. Additionally, interference experiments were conducted to examine the effects of various amino acids on the fluorescence emission intensity of PF-CDs.

To assess the feasibility of the PF-CDs fluorescent probe for detecting PBZ in real samples, the probe was tested with various PBZ concentrations in four homemade apple juice samples. First, the juice samples were centrifuged at 4200 rpm for 5 min to remove residues and then filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter. Different PBZ concentrations (0.8, 6, and 18 μg/L) were added to the PF-CDs system. After thorough mixing of PF-CDs and PBZ, the fluorescence intensity was measured at 345 nm using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

BEAS2B (human normal lung epithelial) and A549 (human lung carcinoma epithelial) cells were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of PF-CDs. First, cells were seeded in separate 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and cultured in RPMI-1640 medium (Servicebio, Serbia) with 5 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, LONSERA, China) and 0.5 % penicillin-streptomycin solution. The cells were incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 12 h. To assess PF-CDs cytotoxicity, cells were treated with 100 μL of culture medium containing varying PF-CDs concentrations (0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 0.1 mg/mL) for 20 h. Afterward, the medium was replaced with 100 μL of 5 % methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) solution and incubated for 4 h to form formazan crystals. The supernatant was removed, and the crystals were dissolved with 150 μL Dimethyl sulfoxide per well. Cell viability was indirectly assessed through absorbance measurement using an enzyme-linked immunoassay detector.

To investigate the cellular uptake of PF-CDs, BMDM cells (mouse bone marrow-derived macrophages) were prepared for fluorescence imaging. The cells were cultured in RPMI medium (Cytiva, USA) supplemented with 5 % FBS (Gibco, USA) and maintained at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2. The BMDM cells were incubated in the PF-CDs system for 24 h. The cells were rinsed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS), then the PBS solution was discarded. Subsequently, 1 mL of 4 % paraformaldehyde was added to fix the cells for 20 min. Fluorescence microscopy images were captured using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Eclipse TiS) under different excitation wavelengths.

Data analysis was performed to ensure the accuracy and reproducibility of the experimental results. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the following formula:
LOD = 3 ×
σ / k
where σ represents the standard deviation of blank measurements, and k denotes the slope of the calibration curve. The limit of quantification (LOQ) was calculated as follows:
LOQ = 10 × LOD

The relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated as:
RSD
%
=
Standard Deviation / Mean
× 100
All data were processed using Origin 2021 software for graphical representation and statistical analysis.

Scheme 1 shows the synthesis procedure and illustrates the mechanism by which PF-CDs detect PBZ. First, PF was dried and pulverized into a fine powder. Subsequently, 0.35 g of the powder was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water and subjected to a microform high-pressure autoclave. PF-CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method at high pressure and temperature. The mass of the obtained PF-CDs was determined to be 99 mg, resulting in a yield of 28.3 %. Upon excitation at 260 nm, these PF-CDs exhibited a distinct emission peak at 345 nm, producing a pale blue fluorescence. However, the introduction of the PBZ pesticide into the PF-CDs solution resulted in fluorescence quenching due to the interaction between PBZ and the fluorescent groups of PF-CDs. This caused a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity. Therefore, PF-CDs can serve as a probe for developing methods for multi-residue pesticide analysis in real samples.

The sub-surface morphological structure, functional groups, and elemental composition of PF-CDs were evaluated using various testing equipment. The FETEM image of PF-CDs (Fig. 1a) reveals a dispersive spherical shape, with diameters ranging from 1.86 to 4.88 nm (n = 220) and an average diameter of 3.41 nm. The HR-FETEM image of PF-CDs (Fig. 1b) displays well-ordered crystal lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.3045 nm, indicating the presence of graphitic structures in PF-CDs. This confirms that PF-CDs form a stable and uniform system.

The functional groups present on CDs were analyzed via FTIR. The FTIR spectra of PF-CDs (Fig. 2a) reveal a broad absorption peak at 3364.84 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, and CH (Carbonaro & Nucara, 2010; Hao et al., 2015; Ţucureanu et al., 2016). The peaks in the 2850–3110 cm−1 range can be attributed to CH stretching vibrations (Mewada et al., 2013; Ţucureanu et al., 2016). The peak at 1612.03 cm−1 is due to NH bending vibrations (Singh et al., 2020). The peak in the 1600–1690 cm−1 range corresponds to CO stretching vibrations (Carbonaro & Nucara, 2010; Wang et al., 2014). The peak at 1516.38 cm−1 is assigned to CC aromatic skeletal vibrations (Mewada et al., 2013; Ţucureanu et al., 2016). The peak at 779.21 cm−1 indicates the absorption of CH out-of-plane bending vibrations, while peaks between 1340 and 1465 cm−1 correspond to CH bending vibrations (Ojeda & Dittrich, 2012; Wu et al., 2013). The peaks in the 1000–1300 cm−1 range are associated with CO stretching vibrations (Ţucureanu et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2014). These findings indicate that the PF-CDs structure comprises nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups, specifically carboxyl and acylamino groups. These functional groups play a crucial role in the stability of PF-CDs in aqueous solutions (Hou et al., 2016).

The XRD pattern of PF-CDs (Fig. 2b) reveals a relatively broad peak around 21.3°, corresponding to the (002) peak of CDs and indicating their amorphous nature (Hu et al., 2022). The peaks at 2θ = 26.7° and 28° may be attributed to impurities, suggesting that the synthesized CDs may contain other doped impurities (Wang et al., 2024).

The XPS spectra of PF-CDs (Fig. 2c) exhibit characteristic peaks at 284.81, 532.71, and 399.98 eV, corresponding to carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s), and nitrogen (N1s), respectively. This indicates that the sample mainly consists of C, O, and N. To precisely identify the elemental forms, the C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra were deconvoluted. The C1s spectra (Fig. 2d) exhibit peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288 eV, corresponding to C–C/C–H, CO, and C–N/C=O bonds (Wu et al., 2013). The high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 2e) mainly feature CO (531.1 eV) and CO bonds (532.7 eV) (Li et al., 2020). Additionally, the N1s spectra (Fig. 2f) display peaks for CN (400 eV) and NH4+ (401.7 eV) groups (Shi et al., 2024). The presence of various C, N, and O groups in CDs enhances their hydrophilicity and stabilizes the surface active sites as chromophores or co-chromophores, thereby improving the fluorescence activity of CDs (Alafeef et al., 2024).

Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of PF-CDs was measured using a fluorescence spectrometer. The fluorescence decay behavior of the CDs solution at an λex of 260 nm is shown in the resolved fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S1). The fluorescence spectrum of the CDs solution exhibits double-exponential decay kinetics with τ1 of 5.12 ns (53.11 %) and τ2 of 2.91 ns (46.89 %). According to the double-exponential simulation, the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs is calculated to be 8.77 ns. These results indicate that PF-CDs exhibit multi-exponential fluorescence decay behavior, consistent with the complex surface states and energy transfer processes typically observed in CDs.

Figs. 3a and b show the fluorescence spectrum of the PF-CDs solution at various excitation wavelengths determined using a fluorospectrophotometer. As the excitation wavelength increases from 240 to 260 nm, the fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs correspondingly increases, while the emission wavelength remains constant. At an excitation wavelength of 260 nm, PF-CDs achieve the highest fluorescence intensity. However, further increases in excitation wavelengths from 270 to 400 nm result in a decrease in fluorescence intensity and a red shift in the emission maximum. The wavelength-dependent response can be attributed to variations in surface energy defects and particle sizes in CDs. This is a common phenomenon in nanostructured materials (Cui et al., 2021; Nair et al., 2025; van Dam et al., 2017). As the excitation wavelength increases, the energy of the absorbed photons decreases, which results in a lower energy emission and a red shift in the fluorescence spectrum. This behavior is consistent with findings from previous studies (Liu, 2020; Yan et al., 2019).

Fig. 3c shows the optimal excitation wavelength (260 nm) and emission wavelength (345 nm) curves of the PF-CDs aqueous solution. At an excitation wavelength of 260 nm, the effect of varying PF concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs was investigated. The fluorescence intensity increases with the precursor concentration of up to 3.5 g/mL, which is identified as the optimal precursor concentration (Fig. 3d). Further details on the optimization of reaction time and reactant quantities are provided in Figs. S2 and S3.

To further examine the fluorescence properties of PF-CDs, the fluorescence intensity was measured at different PF-CDs concentrations (Fig. 4a). The fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs first increases linearly with the PF-CDs concentration. However, at higher PF-CDs concentrations (e.g. 41.25 μg/mL), the fluorescence intensity reaches a peak, and the rate of increase slows down. This behavior can be attributed to the agglomeration of CDs particles and self-quenching effects, which become more pronounced at higher PF-CDs concentrations (Cardoso et al., 2022). At high PF-CDs concentrations, interactions between particles can lead to fluorescence quenching, resulting in reduced luminescence intensity.

To assess the photostability of PF-CDs, an aqueous solution of PF-CDs was continuously irradiated with a xenon lamp in a fluorescence spectrophotometer for 2 h at an excitation wavelength of 260 nm. The spectrum was recorded. The fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs remains significantly unchanged (Fig. 4b).

Moreover, the impact of ionic strength (Fig. 4c) on fluorescence intensity was measured. The concentration of the PF-CDs solution was fixed at 41.25 μg/mL, while the NaCl concentration was increased from 0 to 500 μg/mL. The fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs slightly decreases by 9 % owing to the increased ionic strength, indicating good stability in high-ionic strength environments. Additionally, the study investigated the pH dependence of PF-CDs using BR buffer solutions across a pH range of 1–11 (Fig. 4d). The results reveal that the fluorescence intensity of PF-CDs remains stable in acidic environments but significantly decreases under alkaline conditions. This pH-dependent photoluminescence behavior is commonly attributed to protonation and deprotonation effects on the surface of carbon quantum dots (Chang et al., 2022; Schäferling, 2016). Despite the high fluorescence intensity observed at pH 6–7, a physiological pH of 7.4 was selected as the optimal detection environment to ensure the reliability and relevance of PF-CDs for biological applications.

Before the calibration curve of PBZ is analyzed, determining the optimal experimental conditions is crucial. This includes selecting the optimal concentration of PF-CDs and the appropriate buffer type and dosage to ensure maximum accuracy. Fig. 5a shows the effect of various PF-CDs concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of the PF-CDs-PBZ system, with the PBZ concentration held constant. F0 represents the fluorescence emission intensity, which increases with increasing PF-CDs concentrations (Fig. 5a). F denotes the fluorescence intensity of the PF-CDs-PBZ system after PBZ addition. ∆F (calculated as F0 − F) indicates the degree of fluorescence quenching caused by PBZ on PF-CDs. At a PF-CDs concentration of 41.25 μg/mL, ΔF reaches its maximum, indicating that this concentration is optimal for detecting PBZ.

The effect of 12.5 % of seven buffer solutions with a pH of 7.4 was tested individually on the PF-CDs-PBZ system (Fig. 5b). The Na2HPO4-citric acid buffer solution leads to the maximum fluorescence quenching of PF-CDs by PBZ, indicating that this buffer is the optimal medium for detecting PBZ. The impact of the Na2HPO4-citric acid buffer solution concentration on the fluorescence quenching of the PF-CDs-PBZ system was examined (Fig. 5c). At a Na2HPO4-citric acid buffer concentration of 10 %, the PF-CDs-PBZ system exhibits the highest sensitivity. According to these optimization experiments, the fluorescence emission intensity of PF-CDs at different PBZ concentrations was measured using a pH of 7.4 and a PF-CDs concentration of 41.25 μg/mL.

The quenching rate of the PF-CDs-PBZ system is proportional to its fluorescence intensity across PBZ concentrations ranging from 0.78 to 146.87 μg/L (Fig. 6a). Notably, as the PBZ concentration increases, a blue shift in the fluorescence emission peak is observed. This shift can be attributed to interactions between PBZ and the surface functional groups of PF-CDs, which may alter the electronic environment of CDs. These changes can affect the energy levels of PF-CDs, causing the emission to shift toward shorter wavelengths and higher energy (Cui et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2019).

Fig. 6b shows the linear relationship between ΔF (the photoluminescence quenching value of PF-CDs) and PBZ concentrations of 0.78–18.75 μg/L, indicating excellent linearity. The linear regression equation for PBZ detection is ΔF = 155.9 + 59.9 [Q] (μg/L), with a correlation coefficient of 0.992 and an LOD of 0.005 μg/L. The concentration range of 0.78–18.75 μg/L was selected for the calibration curve owing to its consistent and linear response, ensuring accurate quantification of PBZ in real samples. These results indicate that PF-CDs exhibit stable fluorescence properties, making them an effective fluorescent probe for the quantitative detection of PBZ.

A comparison of the proposed PF-CDs-based sensor with previously reported methods for PBZ detection is presented in Table 1 . The data reveal that the proposed sensor exhibits a lower LOD and a wider linear detection range than previously reported methods. These results highlight the potential of PF-CDs as a cost-effective and highly sensitive alternative to conventional detection methods. The PF-CDs fluorescent probe is highly suitable for the practical detection of PBZ in food samples owing to its strong analyte recognition capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the PF-CDs probe in samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the reliability and applicability of the PF-CDs fluorescent probe for detecting PBZ pesticides in real samples, the PBZ concentration in apple juice samples was determined using the PF-CDs-PBZ system with the standard addition method. The results (Table 2 ) indicate an average recovery rate of 94.06 %–108.56 %, indicating the high accuracy of the PF-CDs-based sensor in complex matrices. The RSD values range from 0.35 % to 12.35 %, suggesting good reproducibility. These findings confirm the applicability of the PF-CDs fluorescent probe for accurately detecting PBZ pesticides in real samples. To further confirm the reliability of the PF-CDs-based sensor, a comparative analysis was performed via HPLC. The results are presented in Table S1. The RSD values for both methods are within acceptable limits, confirming the precision and reliability of the PF-CDs-based sensor.

Additionally, the potential effects of amino acid interference compounds on the fluorescence intensity of the PF-CDs-PBZ system were examined. A 40 μg/mL concentration of each amino acid was used in the experiments, which is comparable to the PF-CDs concentration. The presence of amino acid interference compounds has minimal effect on the detection results (Table 3 ), with relative errors ranging from 1.67 % to 4.78 %. These results indicate that the PF-CDs-PBZ system exhibits strong anti-interference ability, making it suitable for detecting PBZ in complex biological samples. The minimal interference from amino acids further highlights the potential of PF-CDs as a reliable fluorescent probe for practical applications.

PF-CDs exhibit excellent fluorescence properties, making them promising for applications in biochemistry and medicine. Therefore, ensuring the excellent biocompatibility of PF-CDs is crucial. This study aimed to evaluate the biocompatibility of PF-CDs and assess their cytotoxicity through MTT assays.

BEAS2B and A549 cells were used as models to investigate the cytotoxicity of PF-CDs. Cell activity was measured through the MTT colorimetric assay to assess cell survival. After 24 h of incubation with PF-CDs, both BEAS2B and A549 cells exhibit over 90 % viability at concentrations up to 0.1 mg/mL (Figs. 7a and b). This finding indicates that PF-CDs exhibit minimal toxicity, confirming their potential for safe use in biological systems. The performance of PF-CDs is comparable to other CDs-based probes (Wang et al., 2015). Because cytotoxicity is a crucial factor for evaluating biocompatibility, PF-CDs can be safely used as fluorescent probes for PBZ detection in body fluid samples.

BMDM cells were used to assess the potential of PF-CDs for intracellular imaging applications. Fig. 8 presents a fluorescence micrograph of BMDM cells stained with PF-CDs. Fig. 8a shows the bright field image of the BMDM cells treated with PF-CDs, while Fig. 8b displays blue fluorescence images of the cells captured under fluorescence microscopy. The detection of light blue fluorescence indicates that the PF-CDs probe can effectively penetrate the cell membrane during incubation. These results indicate that the PF-CDs nanoprobe can serve as an effective fluorescent probe for biological imaging, highlighting the feasibility of this method. This can be attributed to the small size and surface functional groups of PF-CDs, which facilitate their uptake by cells. The findings are consistent with previous studies. Additionally, the high fluorescence intensity and low cytotoxicity of PF-CDs suggest their potential as versatile biological probes for cell imaging and diagnostics (Du et al., 2019).

This study presents a novel and sustainable approach for synthesizing PF-CDs from the biological waste of PF as a precursor through a one-step hydrothermal method. A series of experiments confirmed that PF-CDs exhibited stable, photostable, and water-soluble fluorescence properties, making them highly suitable for biological and environmental applications. Notably, PF-CDs exhibited excellent sensitivity and selectivity for PBZ detection (a triazole plant growth regulator), with a broad range of 0.78–18.75 μg/L and a low LOD of 0.005 μg/L. These findings highlight the potential of PF-CDs as an effective fluorescent probe for pesticide detection. The practical applicability of PF-CDs was validated through successful PBZ detection in real fruit juice samples, with recovery rates ranging from 100 % to 105 %. Moreover, the biocompatibility of PF-CDs was confirmed through MTT assays and cell imaging experiments, indicating their potential for use in biological systems with minimal cytotoxicity.

To evaluate the environmental friendliness of the synthesis process, the BAGI software was utilized to assess the practicality of the hydrothermal method. The results indicated high efficiency and applicability for real samples, with a practicality score of 67.5. Additionally, the green characteristics of the method were assessed using the AGREEprep tool (Wojnowski et al., 2022). The AGREEprep score was calculated to be 0.79. This indicates the potential of the PF-CDs-based sensor for green applications in environmental monitoring and food safety.

This study provides a green and cost-effective alternative for synthesizing PF-CDs and introduces a highly sensitive and reliable method for detecting PBZ in complex environmental and food samples. The findings have significant implications for improving food safety and environmental monitoring, particularly in pesticide residue analysis. Future research should explore the use of PF-CDs in detecting other pesticides or environmental pollutants and their potential applications in bioimaging and drug delivery systems. Overall, this study contributes to the advancement of sustainable nanomaterials and their applications in analytical chemistry and environmental science.

Yi Zhang: Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Ping Dong: Methodology, Investigation. Nina Sun: Methodology, Investigation. Xu Wang: Validation, Software. Xinshuai She: Validation, Resources. Yongqian Xu: Validation, Software. Qiongzi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Yun Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Higher Education Institutions in Henan Province (Grant No. 25B330001), the Key Scientific Research Projects of Henan Province (No. 222102310688), the 111 Project (No. D20036), and the start-up fund from Xinxiang Medical University (Grant No. 201905).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.144748.
